hallucinations.description: The paper cites PAT-Agent [1] and SLD-Spec [2] as existing works with full descriptions and arXiv identifiers dated 2025. These references are highly likely fabricated/non-verifiable from the materials provided, and the paper relies on them substantively in the introduction and related work. The codebase contains no evidence that these papers were used or that their claims were validated.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper references: "[1] Xinyue Zuo, Yifan Zhang, Hongshu Wang, Yufan Cai, Zhe Hou, Jing Sun, and Jin Song Dong. PAT-Agent: Autoformalization for Model Checking. arXiv:2509.23675, 2025." and "[2] Zehan Chen, Long Zhang, Zhiwei Zhang, JingJing Zhang, Ruoyu Zhou, Yulong Shen, JianFeng Ma, and Lin Yang. SLD-Spec: Enhancement LLM-assisted Specification Generation for Complex Loop Functions via Program Slicing and Logical Deletion. arXiv:2509.09917, 2025."

hallucinations.description: Several additional references appear suspiciously fabricated or incomplete, especially [4] VeCoGen and [6] CodeMirage, which are used as supporting prior work. Reference [5] is also malformed because it omits authors entirely. This indicates the bibliography contains likely hallucinated or unreliable citations.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper references include: "[4] Merlijn Sevenhuijsen, Khashayar Etemadi, and Mattias Nyberg. VeCoGen: Automating Generation of Formally Verified C Code with Large Language Models. arXiv:2411.19275, 2024.", "[6] Vibhor Agarwal, Yulong Pei, Salwa Alamir, and Xiaomo Liu. CodeMirage: Hallucinations in Code Generated by Large Language Models. arXiv:2408.08333, 2024.", and "[5] PanGu-Coder2: Boosting Large Language Models for Code with Ranking Feedback. arXiv:2307.14936, 2024."

hallucinations.description: The paper repeatedly frames SpecBridge as using formal verification tools and inferred formal specifications, including satisfiability verification and solver-style checking. In reality, the implementation performs only test-based execution and very shallow syntax/string checks. No theorem prover, SMT solver, model checker, or true formal verification backend is integrated.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "uses these specifications to guide and verify LLM-generated code"; "Consistency Checking Module validates these specifications through ensemble disagreement detection and satisfiability verification"; "Formal SMT Integration: Incorporate actual SMT solvers (like Z3) for specification verification, rather than relying solely on test cases." Code in verification.py: "def verify_code_with_tests(code: str, test_cases: list, func_name: str)" and "check_specification_satisfiability" merely evals the precondition on provided test cases; there is no SMT/theorem proving integration anywhere in the code.

hallucinations.description: The paper claims the system infers and uses loop invariants and type constraints as part of a structured formal specification tuple. The actual implementation neither enforces nor uses invariants or type constraints during code generation or verification; invariants are only requested in prompts and then ignored downstream.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "Specification Inference Module: Generates formal pre/post-conditions and loop invariants" and "Each specification si is represented as a tuple: si = (Pre_i, Post_i, Inv_i, Types_i)." Code: spec_inference.py returns JSON with keys precondition/postcondition/invariants only; code_generation.py prompt includes only precondition and postcondition; verification.py validates only pre/post syntax and test behavior. No type constraints or invariant checking appear in the code.

hallucinations.description: The disagreement-score equation in the paper is inconsistent with the implementation and is incorrectly normalized. With K candidates and pairwise comparison over i != j, the denominator K(K-1) counts ordered pairs, whereas the implementation compares only unordered pairs i < j and divides by the number of unordered pairs. The paper presents this as if it matches the method in practice, but it does not.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper equation: "D(S_candidates) = 1 - 1/(K(K-1)) sum_{i != j} Equiv(s_i, s_j)". Code in spec_inference.py: "for i in range(len(candidates)): for j in range(i + 1, len(candidates))" and then "agreement_rate = agreement_count / total_pairs; return 1.0 - agreement_rate".

hallucinations.description: The paper defines success rate as a set cardinality divided by N but omits the full denominator condition and does not specify the dependence on each generated code instance cleanly. More importantly, several postconditions/specifications presented as formal expressions are not valid executable or logical formulas, contradicting the paper's framing of them as formal specifications. Examples include quantified English fragments such as 'for all valid i, j' embedded in a Python-like expression and references to undefined functions like decompressed, fib, valid_nesting, and max_consecutive_length.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "formal specifications (pre/post-conditions, invariants)" and equation-style representation. Dataset in data.py contains postconditions such as "result[j][i] == matrix[i][j] for all valid i, j", "decompressed(result) == s", "(n == 0 and result == 0) or (n == 1 and result == 1) or (result == fib(n-1) + fib(n-2))", and "result == max_consecutive_length(nums)".

hallucinations.description: The paper reports figures and analysis that are not actually generated by the experiment code as described, or are contradicted by the repository materials. Most notably, the paper says high ensemble disagreement occurred for 97% of problems (29/30), while the README reports 90% ambiguous. This inconsistency suggests at least one set of reported experimental claims was not faithfully derived from a single validated run.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "97% of problems (29/30) exceeded the ambiguity threshold of 0.5." README.md: "High ensemble disagreement (90% ambiguous) suggests specification inference challenges."

hallucinations.description: The paper states that the benchmark consists of 'a curated benchmark of 30 coding problems spanning three difficulty levels' with 15 easy, 14 medium, and 1 hard, and presents this as an evaluation benchmark. In reality, the dataset is hand-written directly in data.py rather than an external benchmark, and many included 'formal specifications' are ad hoc, inconsistent, or not machine-checkable. This overstates the rigor of the benchmark and the specification quality.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "We evaluate SpecBridge on a curated benchmark of 30 coding problems..." Code file data.py manually defines BENCHMARK_PROBLEMS = [...] with 30 tasks and many informal/non-executable spec strings such as "for all valid i, j" and undefined helper predicates/functions.

overall_assessment: Hallucinations were found. The paper’s core experimental summary numbers largely match the provided log, but the manuscript overclaims the formality of the method, contains inconsistent and mathematically mismatched methodology descriptions, and includes multiple likely fabricated or unreliable citations. Severity is moderate: the main results seem partly grounded in code/logs, but the scholarly framing and some technical claims are not trustworthy.
